title: Symlink resolution loop has no cycle guard and can spin indefinitely
reasoning: The while loop on line 10 follows symlinks iteratively but has no upper bound on iterations. If the symlink chain contains a cycle (e.g. A → B → A, which can occur from a misconfigured install or a malicious PATH/bin directory), the loop never terminates, hanging the CLI invocation indefinitely. Standard hardened wrappers add a counter (e.g. max 40 hops) or use `realpath`/`readlink -f` which detect cycles internally.
recommendation: Add a hop counter (e.g. `local hops=0; while [ -L "$SOURCE" ] && [ $hops -lt 40 ]; do ... hops=$((hops+1)); done`) and exit with an error message if the limit is exceeded, or replace the manual loop with `realpath -- "$SOURCE"` (available on macOS 12.3+ and all Linux).
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: No tests are included for this feature (tests array is empty), and the symlink-resolution path is only exercised at runtime via the bin wrapper.
suggestedRegressionTest: Create a temporary circular symlink pair, invoke the wrapper, and assert it exits non-zero within a timeout rather than hanging.
minimumFixScope: Add an iteration cap of ~40 to the while loop on line 10 and emit a diagnostic to stderr before exiting 1 when exceeded.
